title: Kilburn's First Program
lesson:
This is the one that made history. The very first program ever run on the Baby, on 21 June 1948, was written by Tom Kilburn to find the highest proper factor of a number. It tried every candidate from the number downward, checking each one for divisibility, using nothing but repeated subtraction. For 2 to the 18th power (262,144) it ground through about 3.5 million operations in 52 minutes, and it got the right answer: 131,072. That run is the dawn of practical software.

Testing for divisibility is really just testing the remainder. A number is divisible by D exactly when (number mod D) is 0. And on a subtract-only machine, you find a remainder the obvious way: keep subtracting D until subtracting once more would go negative, and the last value you had is the remainder. That little routine is the beating heart of Kilburn's factor program.

Your capstone is that routine. Keep subtracting D from N, remembering the previous value each time. When the subtraction finally goes negative, the value you remembered is the remainder. (Since the Baby can't even copy a word directly, you copy by negating twice: LDN, STO, LDN, the trick you've used all level.)
prompt: Work out 20 mod 6 by repeated subtraction, the core of the world's first program. Track the previous value, and when subtracting 6 would go negative, that previous value (2) is the remainder. Leave it in the accumulator and STP.
starterCode:
; remainder = N mod D, by repeated subtraction.
; Each pass: prev = rem, then rem = rem - 6. If rem goes negative, the answer is prev.
loop:   LDN rem      ; A = -rem
        STO t        ; t = -rem
        LDN t        ; A = rem
        STO prev     ; prev = rem
        LDN rem      ; A = -rem
        STO t
        LDN t        ; A = rem
        SUB six      ; A = rem - 6
        STO rem      ; rem = rem - 6
        CMP          ; if rem < 0, skip the JMP
        JMP loop
        LDN prev     ; recover prev (the remainder)
        STO t
        LDN t
        STP
rem:    NUM 20
six:    NUM 6
prev:   NUM 0
t:      NUM 0
hints: A remainder is what's left when you can't subtract D one more time without going negative.
Save 'prev = rem' at the top of each pass, then subtract. When rem goes negative, prev holds the answer.
To copy a value on a subtract-only machine: LDN it (negate), STO to a scratch word, then LDN the scratch (negate again).
The values go 20, 14, 8, 2, then -4 (too far). The last value before going negative, 2, is 20 mod 6.
successText: A must equal 2 (that's 20 mod 6), found by repeated subtraction.
reference:
loop: LDN rem
STO t
LDN t
STO prev
LDN rem
STO t
LDN t
SUB six
STO rem
CMP
JMP loop
LDN prev
STO t
LDN t
STP
rem: NUM 20
six: NUM 6
prev: NUM 0
t: NUM 0
